sentences.text: Oh, that took place very early after Hitler came in power.
It-- it must have-- it was in 1933.
I think it was shortly before Christmas, because I seem to remember Christmas trees and such, children with toys.
I think so.
But I know it was-- it seemed-- it seemed like the first year that Hitler was in power.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, he was not a cousin.
He was a-- he was an uncle.
I remember calling him Uncle Jacob, which would be a Jacob in English.
His name was Jacob, and he lived across from Frankfurt in Sachsenhausen.
That was like across from the Main River.
And we had-- we had heard about it.
And my-- he was an uncle on my father's side, and my parents were very upset and visited him.
And he was all right later, but it was a very-- it was not a very nice incident, something that was very depressing.
I believe-- I-- I don't know exactly the year, but it was in-- I don't think it was in '33.
I think it may have been 1934.
I couldn't say for sure the date.
But I seem to remember it must have been about 1934. '26, '36, I must have been about-- in 1934, I was-- '31, about 12, 13 years old.
And that's about the time when the-- when the newspaper came out.
Oh, I don't remember that.
I do remember seeing it, and people bought it in newspaper stores.
And it was-- sometimes it was just lying around in one of these public places, post office.
I don't remember exactly how it was distributed.
But I remember whenever I saw it, it was-- I was ashamed almost to walk on the street.
I was always ashamed.
I was ashamed to be Jewish.
There was anti-Semitism, but not in such a way.
I really never experienced that.
It wasn't really that serious.
We kind of took it for granted, but really not much.
I don't remember that much anti-Semitism when Hitler came in-- before Hitler came in power.
Maybe I felt it a little more because my parents spoke Yiddish, and I was always a little bit ashamed of the fact that they spoke Yiddish.
They were looked down a little bit because of that.
But I don't know whether it was because they were Jewish or because of their Yiddish language.
They-- I would-- I have to say that they also learned to speak German later on.
But it was that-- that Yiddish language that made me feel a little bit ashamed.
I'm-- I feel terribly sorry about that now, because in America the Yiddish language is a very respected language, but it wasn't so respected in Germany.
Well, as I mentioned before, it was-- wasn't so easy to go to school.
And in 1935, my parents decided that-- well, there was only my brother and myself at that time visiting school.
My brother had left-- I don't remember, maybe in 1933 already school, because he needed to take a job.
Times were getting bad, and he had to bring in a little money.
But I know that I left school in 1935, mostly because walking to school and coming home was so bad, and my parents were very scared that something would happen to me.
And they decided to-- not just myself.
Many children would just not continue their education.
They would just leave.
We attended, as I mentioned before, this philantropene.
And many times, as coming out of the philantropene, you would have children waiting there for you just to bother you, or hit you, or throw stones.
And that's when my parents decided that I would discontinue my education, which was a very hard thing.
And not only for educational purposes, but I had my friends in school.
And-- but at that time, quite a few children, quite a few students, would leave school already.

What I did then, then I started to learn some kind of a-- of a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something that you do with your hand, either dressmaking-- I used to be-- I used to-- a trade, that's what I was thinking for.
I used to be good in sewing.
And I would sit home and do-- and sew dresses for my mother, sometimes for friends, for myself.
And-- and at that time, in 1935 the situation became already serious.
It was the time when I remember my parents taking everything a little bit more serious and saying, wait a minute, Hitler is not going to go away that soon.
He's been now in power for two years and it doesn't look that he's-- on the contrary, he seems to be getting more popular as he goes on.
And people-- already some people already started leaving Germany.
Jews started leaving Germany.
And I think it was around 1935 that my parents realized that the situation was bad.

I would also like to mention that in 1933, when Hitler came in power, my oldest sister, who was married, her father-in-law had decided before Hitler came in power that he will not stay in Germany if he becomes the leader-- the Fuhrer, as they said in Germany.
And he took his family, including my sister, of course, to France.
They went to France on a visit.
He thought that, at that time-- again, in '33, many people thought he wouldn't last very long.
But so he said-- he left everything.
He went to France with his wife.
He had another son, the son that my sister was married to.
And supposedly for six months, and-- but he stayed longer.
My sister still lives in France.
I just want to mention so.
And in 1935, my other sister, the second one, got married to someone in Munich, to a man in Munich.
And she moved to Munich in 1935.
So that left my middle sister, my brother, and myself in the house.
To get back now to the schooling, right, I left school in 1935.
Many other Jewish children did too, but some of us stayed.
The school, the philantropene, did not close yet at that time.
But many Jewish children did not visit school anymore.
Regina.
Mary.
Right, went to Munich.
I can't-- I don't remember that.
Because the grades seemed so different that what is in America.
I seem to remember-- I don't think we started with first grade.
I would have to think about that.
I don't know what grade that was.
But I could probably figure out it would be similar to-- in 1935, I was about 14 years old.
Yes.
That was the-- I think it was in 1936.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father, as far as I remember, I learned it that very day, came home one day and said that he had been going for a new permit.
Somehow they needed-- each year he needed some kind of a permit to continue his business.
I don't know if this was just for Jews or if everybody had to do that.
But he needed some kind of a permit.
And he came home.
I'll never forget that day.
And he was very pale, and he locked the door of the business.
You know, I told you that we had-- our apartment was right behind the business.
And we saw him come, and he locked the door.
And he said he can't continue working anymore.
And my mother said, why?
He said, he did not get his permission.
He was told that they are not going to continue any more permits for Polish Jews.
In other words, he was not allowed to work anymore.
So what he did was he had a little closet in that store.
It was like you couldn't really walk in, but you could-- he kept his tools in there.
And he hid the closet by wallpapering the store.
I remember it was very full of flowery wallpaper, and he wallpapered the closet door so that it wouldn't be noticeable.
And what he did was then he-- he locked the door.
He would draw the blinds, or whatever they had at that time, and he would work.
And we children would sit by the window in the next room and to watch.
And when we saw the Nazis come-- well, you know, they were easy to hear, because they wore these high boots.
When we heard the sound of those boots, we would warn him.
And he would take his tools-- you know, he was working.
He would take his tools and everything and throw it into that little closet.
And we would sit and sew or do anything.
And he would have a newspaper on his lap so that when they did come in, which happened every once in a while, he would-- he didn't work.
They checked up on him, and we were always afraid that they would check.
Because if they would have seen him working, they would take him away right away.

And what he did for us in order to make a living-- he still had to make a living-- he would-- in the evening when it got dark, he would put on a big overcoat and hide the bags, the leather bags, under his overcoat and visit his customers and sell some of these bags, what we call under the table, so to say.
So this is how he made his living.
And meanwhile, my sister brought in some money.
She was repairing rugs, the sister, my middle sister, and I was sewing.
I-- I made alterations for people.
And my brother also had a job in Frankfurt in a-- in an office.
By the Nazis?
I really have to think about that.
I think so.
Yes, I think so.
Because they were not just Jews, they were Jews that came to Germany.
And yes, I would say that they were treated worse than the German Jews around that time.
Later on, everybody was treated the same as a Jew.
But I'm talking now about starting with 1933, and all the way up to maybe 1937.
And then everybody was treated alike.
Well, I seem-- I remember that they started taking the situation seriously when my father couldn't continue working, because then times got very bad.
They had a very hard time.
They just didn't have enough money to pay their rent, to pay for food.
We all pitched in, but it wasn't enough.
And that's when they said that we have to go someplace.
And they were always-- they were, where can we go?
They started going to the American consulate to take out number, a quota number.
That was the first thing, because they did want to go to America.
That was their country.
They were thinking of going to Palestine.
I don't know why they didn't.
In those times, it seemed very hard.
But they were hoping to go to the United States.
As a matter of fact, there's a-- a little something happy that I have to-- that I just remember.
As I said before, our maiden name was Ebe.
And my brother was always kind of trying to-- to change the situation to make us laugh.
So he always used to say to my mother, you know, Ma, when we go to America, you'd be Mississippi-- Mrs.
Ebe.
And he used to say Mississippi.
I just-- I remember this right now.
That's when we-- and she laughed when he said that.
But yes, at the time, it was getting-- they were seriously thinking of emigrating, and then they tried.
And as a matter of fact, they-- what we did then, my brother once brought home a telephone book from New York.
I don't remember-- he found it someplace.
He came home with this heavy book.
We have never seen a telephone book like that in our life.
And what we did, we were all sitting around that book and looking for Jewish names-- Levy, Horowitz, all the Jewish names, Israel.
And we wrote, we spent most of the little money that we had writing letters to Americans begging them to give us an affidavit.
You needed an affidavit in order to immigrate to America.
You needed a quota number.
You needed-- you needed to know someone who would give you an affidavit, which meant putting away a certain amount of money, I think, for one or two years.
As far as I remember, that's how it was.
And so we always said that we will not be-- we all have professions.
And we told them we can sew, and we can do all kinds of things.
And-- and we begged, and we even enclosed a reply-- a reply stamp, something like that, so it wouldn't cost them any money.
We had a few responses but no affidavits.
So we were really-- and this went on into '37.
But in '37, the situation of getting into a-- you could get out of Germany.
But it seems that one country after the other closed their doors, because meanwhile, the Jewish-- many of the Jewish people had gone to other countries.
And-- and I guess many of the countries realized that they're getting a lot of Jewish people.
And I remember it was very hard to go any place.
I remember my mother saying, I-- I'll go to the jungle.
If I could just go anyplace and just live on bread and water.
But it was-- we just couldn't go any place.
We had no place to go to.
Especially in 1937, it was-- we-- we tried very hard to-- to go away to any place, whoever would want to take us.
We could not get in.
We could get out, but we couldn't get in.
Any changes in the--
Oh, well, in the laws about-- for Jews?
Well, there were always changes.
There was always something new coming in.
Well, the first change was the fact that he wasn't allowed to work openly.
But yes, they had a hard time getting kosher meats.
They didn't allow to-- to-- the meat to be koshered.
And-- and that reminds me of something else also.
I remember being in some kind of a market where my mother used to bought the chicken.
They had live chickens then, and there was this what they call [YIDDISH], the person who would kill the chicken.
And I remember being there in the market, and somebody came in, a non-Jewish man, big, non-Jewish man.
And he said, your heads will roll like the heads of these chickens roll.
I'll never forget that.
I remember I couldn't sleep for a long time after I heard him say that.
And that just-- just came to me as you asked me that.
But there are other laws.
At this point, I don't remember.
I remember being-- not being able to work because we couldn't get the kosher meat.
And times were just getting very bad.
And then meanwhile, relatives and friends would leave.
We already heard people leave.
And we were-- as happy as we were for them, it was like going to the street and you met another Jewish person, you would say, where are you going?
So do you have anything?
We were always so interested in finding out.
And sometimes people said, I don't know what we are going to do.
We tried this country.
We tried that country.
Well, sometimes people would say, yes, we are leaving tomorrow for such and such a place.
And-- and as happy as we were for these people, we always wished that we would be in their places.
That's how-- how we wanted to leave.
Because now, by now, by 1937, there were so many bad incidents.
A neighbor was picked up.
They said that he was-- he was giving out communist-- he made communist propaganda.
He was giving out leaflets for-- to be communist, and they picked him up.
Whether it was true or not, I don't know, but he disappeared.
People disappeared, and you didn't know where to.
And we were afraid that they would come for us also.
Mainly, mainly we were all making sure that we learned a trade.
My parents said, you have to be-- you can't be just a dressmaker, you've got to be a good dressmaker.
And-- and she said-- they said the same thing to the others.
My brother-- you can't be just an office person.
You've got to do something that you can do in another country.
You've got to learn something that will enable you to make money in another country.
And so, well, at this-- at that time, there were only three of us in the house, as I mentioned before.
So that was-- that was the main thing.
And these were actually the preparations that they made.

They were always concerned with just trying to get out and making sure that they-- they weren't so concerned about-- my father was-- he was able to do most anything, being in the leather bag business.
He also said that he'll go as a shoemaker.
He'll-- he'll sew shoes.
He was able to do that.
He had-- he was able to do many things.
He said he can be a painter.
He can be a wallpaper hanger.
So that was the biggest concern, not just in our house, with everybody.
Everybody was trying to-- there were doctors and dentists who suddenly became shoe solers.
And you know, they learned-- it was the trade you had to learn in anticipation of getting into another country, because nobody spoke the language.
Very few people spoke the language of whatever country you intended to go to.
And that was on our mind, just getting out.
And I'm talking now about 1937.
By that time, everybody wanted to get out.
OK.
Were you still able to attend synagogue at that point?
To attend?
Yes, yes.
Synagogue we always attended.
Yes, we always attended synagogue, yes.
We would go to the synagogue, even if it was a little hard.
Sometimes going out, they would call us Jew, you know, this dirty Jew, we got so used to it that we hardly heard it.
Hey, dirty Jew, you're going to the synagogue.
You know, we heard that.
But yes, we always attended synagogue.
That was-- became more important, you know?
You were hoping that God will help you.
Well, actually, next came-- in 1938, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- times were bad.
And we had heard of many incidents-- people being arrested, and for no reason.
And Jews getting hurt in one way or another.
But in 1938, we had the experience on October 28.
There were five of us in the house, my parents and, as I mentioned before, and the three-- and my sister, brother, and myself.
And that day, on October 28 at 5 o'clock in the morning, five SS men knocked on the door very heavily, pushed open the door, and came in and shouted, get dressed.
You're-- you're being deported.
Just get out.
They didn't even say you're being deported.
Get dressed and get out of here.
And I remember my parents, what is-- what for?
And then they said, you're going to be deported.
And said, just get dressed.
We give you five minutes.
And we all got dressed.
And we-- my mother tried to make some tea, something warm.
It was cold and a rainy day.
And we were ready in a few minutes.
And-- and they said nothing.
My mother-- can we take something with us?
No, not a thing.
We couldn't take anything with us.
And we walked out.
It was a rainy day, and there was a big truck outside with-- with men, women, and children.
And we got into the truck, and-- and they-- and they took us-- they picked up more people, more Jewish people.
I remember my mother couldn't get the steps up quick enough, and he pushed her up, and she hurt her leg.
And they took us to a prison, and they pushed us into a prison cell.
And we stayed there all day, and they kept on pushing more, and more, and more people into that cell until we hardly could breathe anymore.
And that was early in the morning.
And then in the evening, they-- they let us get out, and we-- we had to get into a truck, that infamous truck.
I remember high truck.
You had to climb up these high steps to the truck.
And they took us to the railway station, the Frankfurt Hauptbahnhof, and there were many other people there.
And we had to get-- there was a train.
We had to get into the train.
And only then did we find out.
I don't remember.
I seem to remember that only then did we find out that we are going to be deported to Poland.
That's what I remember.
I don't know, maybe my-- the rest of my family knew.
But I remember it was at that railway station that I learned we are going to be deported to Poland.
Maybe from other Jews.
You know, from-- I don't remember.
I know that I thought, where are they deporting us?
Where are we going?
These were only Polish Jews.
This was a Polish Jewish deportation.
It was one of-- I believe it was one of the first deportations that took place.
I don't remember that Jews were deported in such masses until that time.
Maybe they were, but not that I know of.
And this was a-- the first deportation was only Jews.
And-- and we had to get into the train.
And then there was a train ride that seemed like forever, and we had nothing to eat or drink.
We had eat the last time on Thursday evening, the night before we were picked up.
And it was-- and then we had been all day long in that prison, in that cell, and then we were in that train.
There was no food and no water.
And we all took turns [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] fainting, fainting.
You know, we all fainted at one time or another.
And then I remember it took-- seemed to take forever.
And I remember one thing.
On that train, I thought by myself, are there people out there that were happy, and singing, and dancing, and we are on a train being deported?
And I remember that, sitting in that train thinking if there are people that are really happy today, because we are in such a terrible situation.
And we were deported.
It took quite a while.
And then the-- the train stopped many times.
But at one stop, they said-- they ordered, out, everybody out.
And we had-- and then there  came all kinds of orders, they-- the SS men with the rifle.
They would-- and then they ordered, they said, in one row.
They put like six or seven people, five to seven people in one row, one behind the other.
I would think there were about 1,500 people.
I seem to remember about 1,500-- not remember, but it seems to me now looking back that there were about 1,500 people.
So we were all like six abreast, one behind the other, holding hands very tightly.
No one wanted to get lost from the family.
And then they ordered, march, march.
And we marched, and they were-- the SS men were walking next to us with their goose steps, and with the rifles pointing at us at all times.
And everybody was marching.
We were marching and marching for-- for a-- for quite a while.
And then there came a time when they had-- when they ordered us-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
When they ordered us-- when they shouted that there will be the last German-- what do you call that-- German-- I need a little time to think  now.
Can I do that?
Thank you.
